name: Connector Isolation & Throttling
strategicIntent: Enforce connector-level rate-limiting, backpressure, and failover patterns to protect both internal systems and external partners from traffic spikes, cascading failures, and resource exhaustion.
problemStatement: Integration connectors without throttling controls allow traffic spikes from one system to overwhelm downstream targets, causing cascading failures across the integration layer. A single misbehaving connector can degrade the entire integration platform.

useCases.use: SAP RFC connectors that must respect legacy system concurrency limits
useCases.dontUse: Low-volume internal API calls between co-located microservices

useCases.use: Retailer EDI connectors processing seasonal order surges
useCases.dontUse: Asynchronous event consumers with built-in consumer group scaling

useCases.use: Payment gateway connectors subject to external rate limits and SLA penalties
useCases.dontUse: Azure-native service integrations with auto-scaling capabilities

components: Azure Service Bus — request buffering with session-based ordering
Azure API Management — connector-level rate-limiting and quota policies
Azure Functions (Durable) — backpressure orchestration and retry scheduling
Azure Cache for Redis — token bucket / sliding window rate-limiter state
Azure Monitor — throttling metrics, connector health scores, and alerting
Azure Traffic Manager — connector failover routing to standby instances
interactionSteps: Inbound request arrives at connector endpoint via API Management
Rate-limiter checks Redis-backed token bucket for connector-specific quota
If within quota, request is forwarded to connector for processing
If quota exceeded, request is buffered in Service Bus for deferred processing
Backpressure orchestrator (Durable Functions) processes buffered requests at safe rate
If connector fails health check, Traffic Manager routes to standby connector instance
Azure Monitor tracks throttling rate, queue depth, and connector response times
securityConsiderations: Rate-limiter state in Redis must be protected with authentication and encryption in transit
Buffered requests in Service Bus must retain original authentication context
Connector failover must not bypass security controls applied to the primary instance
Throttling configurations must be protected from unauthorized modification
All throttling events must be logged for security audit and capacity planning

guardrails.rule: Every connector must have rate-limiting configured with documented thresholds
Backpressure buffering must be implemented for connectors to rate-limited backends
Connector health checks must be configured with automated failover
Throttling rate and queue depth must be monitored with alerting thresholds
Rate-limit thresholds must be reviewed quarterly with capacity planning team

exceptionProcess: Submit Integration Architecture Board request for connectors without throttling. Must include downstream system capacity analysis and peak load testing results demonstrating no degradation risk.

revisionHistory.author: Raj Patel
revisionHistory.summary: Initial connector throttling pattern with token bucket rate-limiting

revisionHistory.author: Raj Patel
revisionHistory.summary: Added backpressure orchestration, failover routing, and seasonal capacity guidance
